topic: A student learns their scholarship application was denied
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. My hands, already clammy, shook so violently I had to grip the edge of my desk until my knuckles were white. I reread the subject line, the words blurring into an incomprehensible smear: "Scholarship Application Update." Each word seemed to hammer against my chest, a dull, crushing weight. I kept blinking, certain the screen was mocking me, refusing to show the harsh reality of the rejection. My breath hitched, shallow and rapid, catching in my throat like shards of glass. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of metal on linoleum sounding deafening in the sudden silence of my small room. I wanted to run, to disappear into the shadows, but my feet felt rooted to the spot, heavy and unresponsive. I stared at the ceiling, tracing the cracks in the paint with my gaze, unable to form a coherent thought beyond the echoing emptiness where hope used to reside.

[story 2]

"No." The single word echoed in the cavern of my mind, amplified by the hollow sound of my own voice when I finally managed to whisper it aloud. I stood frozen in the doorway of the library, the fluorescent lights buzzing overhead casting a sickly, cold glow on my face. My heart was performing a frantic drum solo against my ribs, a wild, uneven rhythm that threatened to spill out. I clutched the printed rejection letter so tightly that the edges dug painfully into my palm. I could feel a sudden, alarming pressure building behind my eyes, a dizzying sensation that made the world tilt slightly. Every sound—the rustle of papers, the distant chatter of students—became sharp, intrusive signals demanding my attention, none of which I could process. I took a staggered step backward, bumping against a bookshelf, and for a moment, the air seemed to thin, leaving me breathless and utterly exposed.

[story 3]

I sat at the kitchen table, the lukewarm coffee forgotten, the paper spread out before me like an accusation. My gaze kept darting back and forth between the denial notice and the empty space across the room, as if the answer were hiding there, waiting to strike. A cold sweat broke out across my forehead, soaking the collar of my shirt. My mouth felt impossibly dry, a desert stretching between my lips. I tried to swallow, but my throat constricted, making the simple act of breathing feel like an immense effort. My entire body was rigid, coiled tight with an internal tremor that radiated outward. I squeezed my eyes shut, counting backward from ten, a futile attempt to anchor myself against the swirling chaos inside. When I opened them, the stark white of the wall seemed too bright, too unforgiving, and I felt a profound, sickening lurch in my stomach.
